title: Writing - Part 2
description: Choose one of the following writing tasks and write your answer in 140-190 words.

tasks.title: Review
tasks.prompt:
You see this notice on a music website:

'Live music reviews wanted! Write a review of a live concert or music festival you have attended. Tell us about the performance, the atmosphere, and the overall experience — and say whether you would recommend it to other music fans.'

Write your review.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Glastonbury Festival — A Once-in-a-Lifetime Musical Experience

Having attended Glastonbury for the first time this summer, I can now fully understand why it occupies such a legendary place in British cultural life. Simply put, it was one of the most extraordinary experiences of my life.

The scale of the festival is difficult to convey in words. Across five days and dozens of stages, the sheer variety of music on offer — from headline rock acts to jazz, folk, electronic, and world music — is staggering. Every walk between stages felt like a discovery, with brilliant performances happening continuously.

The atmosphere is genuinely unique: warm, inclusive, and characterised by a shared sense of adventure that I have never encountered anywhere else. Even the inevitable mud and long queues become part of the experience rather than a deterrent.

Admittedly, tickets are expensive and must be booked many months in advance. The crowds can also be overwhelming for those unaccustomed to large events.

Nevertheless, if you love music and want to experience it in the most immersive way imaginable, Glastonbury is absolutely worth every effort. I am already planning my return.

tasks.title: Email
tasks.prompt:
Your English friend Casey has expressed an interest in learning a musical instrument but is unsure whether to commit to it. Write an email to Casey encouraging them to start learning, explaining the benefits of playing an instrument, addressing their concerns about the time and effort involved, and recommending how to get started.

Write your email to Casey.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Hi Casey,

It is brilliant that you are thinking about learning an instrument — honestly, I think it is one of the best decisions you could make, and I say that as someone who very nearly talked myself out of it before I started learning guitar three years ago.

I completely understand the concern about time. But learning an instrument does not require hours of daily practice. Even fifteen to twenty minutes a day will produce noticeable progress within weeks, and once you start hearing yourself improve, the motivation takes care of itself.

The benefits are genuinely remarkable. Playing music reduces stress, improves concentration and memory, and gives you a form of creative expression that is unlike anything else. Many people also find it a wonderful way to meet others through bands, orchestras, or informal jam sessions.

My advice would be to start with the instrument you find most appealing — that enthusiasm will carry you through the difficult early stages. Excellent free beginner tutorials are available on YouTube for almost every instrument, so you can try before committing to lessons.

Just begin — you will not regret it!

Best,
Sam

tasks.title: Article
tasks.prompt:
You see this notice in an English-language magazine:

'Music in our lives: Write an article about the role that music plays in everyday life. Why is music so important to people, and how does it affect our mood, our memories, and our sense of identity?'

Write your article.
tasks.modelAnswer:
The Invisible Companion: Why Music Matters More Than We Realise

There are very few moments in daily life that music does not accompany. We wake to it, commute with it, exercise to it, and recall some of our most significant memories through it. Yet we rarely stop to consider quite how profoundly it shapes our inner lives.

Science offers some illuminating explanations. Music activates the brain's reward system in ways remarkably similar to food and human connection, releasing dopamine in response to pleasurable sounds. It is also uniquely powerful at triggering autobiographical memory — a particular song can transport us instantly to a specific moment in the past with an emotional vividness that words alone cannot achieve.

Beyond its psychological effects, music plays a central role in shaping identity. The genres we love and the playlists we curate are expressions of who we are. Music connects us to communities of like-minded people and gives us a language for emotions that are difficult to articulate.

In a world of constant noise and distraction, music remains one of humanity's most reliable sources of comfort, inspiration, and joy.
